name: Tangaroa
description: Tangaroa, also known as Tagaloa in some Polynesian cultures, is one of the major gods in Polynesian mythology. He is the god of the sea, creation, and fertility. In many traditions, he is considered the origin of all things, emerging from the primordial darkness (Po) or the cosmic egg. He is responsible for the creation of the islands and all life within the ocean. Tangaroa's power lies in his control over the vast ocean depths, its creatures, and its resources. He is often depicted as a powerful and wise figure, respected and feared by both mortals and other gods. He is also associated with the growth of plants and the abundance of food. He is a central figure in the creation narratives of many Polynesian cultures, showcasing his pivotal role in shaping the world and its inhabitants. His influence extends beyond the physical realm, encompassing spiritual and ancestral connections to the sea. Tangaroa is a symbol of life, abundance, and the interconnectedness of all things within the Polynesian worldview. Legends tell of his battles with other gods, shaping the landscape and establishing the natural order. He is a powerful ancestor and guardian of the Polynesian people.
subtitle: God of the Sea and Creation

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the World
keyMyths.description: Tangaroa breaks free from his shell in the void, beginning the creation of the world. He then creates the other gods and the land.

keyMyths.title: Tangaroa and Tāwhirimātea
keyMyths.description: A conflict arises between Tangaroa, the god of the sea, and Tāwhirimātea, the god of storms, leading to continuous battles and shaping the environment. This feud separates land and sea.

keyMyths.title: The Origin of Fish
keyMyths.description: Tangaroa is responsible for populating the sea with fish and other marine life. He ensures the ocean is abundant and provides for the people.

sources.author: Robert D. Craig

sources.text: Comprehensive overview of Polynesian deities and myths.
